zones.name: Claude Client
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Perfect cohesion with 15 files suggests a well-designed client library with clear responsibilities
Zero coupling indicates proper encapsulation and minimal external dependencies
Comprehensive feature set covering API providers, authentication, CLI integration, and token usage monitoring
Exemplary client library design with perfect cohesion and encapsulation
Demonstrates adapter pattern with multiple providers (API, CLI) unified behind consistent interface
Clean adapter pattern implementation allowing swappable Claude API providers while maintaining consistent client interface
Token usage monitoring in token-usage.ts suggests this zone has dual responsibility as both client library and usage analytics service
Auth module implements multiple authentication strategies but lacks clear strategy pattern interface documentation
token-usage.ts mixing client concerns with analytics creates implicit service responsibility - consider extracting to dedicated analytics zone

zones.name: Hench
insights: 10 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
Good cohesion (0.71) indicates well-structured agent architecture with logical groupings
Moderate coupling (0.29) reflects reasonable interdependencies for core agent functionality
Ten entry points suggest multiple specialized interfaces for analysis, planning, and execution phases
Well-architected agent core with strong cohesion and manageable coupling
Acts as central nervous system with structured lifecycle management across analysis, planning, and execution phases
Heavy bidirectional coupling with cli-infrastructure (30 total imports) suggests agent logic bleeding into CLI concerns
Analysis, planning, and lifecycle management are fundamentally different concerns that should be architecturally separated
Token management logic scattered across lifecycle files creates maintenance burden and testing complexity
Zone mixes three distinct architectural layers: analysis (review, summary), planning (brief, context), and lifecycle (loops, token management) that should be separate zones
Token budget and usage tracking logic duplicated across lifecycle files instead of centralized service
Token budget files (token-budget.ts, token-usage.ts) in lifecycle subfolder create implicit coupling between execution flow and resource management concerns
Analysis subfolder (review.ts, stuck.ts, summary.ts) implements post-execution analysis rather than core agent logic, suggesting architectural layer confusion
Planning subfolder contains brief.ts and context.ts which are pre-execution concerns mixed with lifecycle management
Token management files in lifecycle/ subfolder violate single responsibility - extract to dedicated resource-management zone
Analysis subfolder implements post-execution concerns mixed with core logic - separate into analysis-reporting zone
Move packages/hench/src/tools/rex.ts to dedicated shared-tools zone to eliminate hub dependency pattern across 3 zones
Extract packages/hench/src/tools/rex.ts into shared-interfaces zone to break hub dependency pattern affecting zones hench, hench-3, and hench-4

zones.name: Hench 2
insights: Good cohesion (0.67) shows focused responsibility around task completion and validation
Moderate coupling (0.33) indicates proper integration with Rex task management system
Test coverage across completion validation demonstrates quality engineering practices
Well-focused zone with good cohesion and comprehensive test coverage
Implements command pattern with validation pipeline ensuring task state integrity
Command pattern with validation pipeline provides robust task completion workflow with proper state management
Validation logic split between validation/ subfolder and completion.ts creates inconsistent responsibility boundaries
Test files follow different naming convention (completion-validation.test.ts vs rex.test.ts) indicating unclear testing strategy
validation/completion.ts and agent/completion.ts create split validation responsibility - consolidate validation logic in single location
Test naming inconsistency between completion-validation.test.ts and rex.test.ts suggests unclear testing conventions

zones.name: Hench 3
insights: Strong cohesion (0.77) indicates well-organized CLI and infrastructure components
Low coupling (0.23) shows good separation from core agent logic while maintaining necessary integration
Seven entry points provide comprehensive CLI command surface and configuration management
Excellent CLI architecture with strong cohesion and minimal coupling
Exhibits inverted dependency pattern where CLI commands depend on abstractions rather than concrete implementations
Bidirectional coupling with agent-core may indicate CLI commands directly manipulating agent internals instead of using clean interfaces
CLI commands directly import agent lifecycle components bypassing proper service abstractions
Error handling and configuration logic embedded within CLI layer instead of dedicated services
CLI commands import packages/hench/src/agent/lifecycle/loop.ts directly, violating layer separation and creating tight coupling
Error handling logic embedded in CLI layer instead of using domain service abstractions
Constants.ts in commands subfolder suggests configuration mixed with command implementation rather than centralized constant management
Loop.ts in agent/lifecycle imported by CLI violates expected dependency direction - CLI should not orchestrate agent internals directly
Error handling in cli/errors.ts is domain-specific rather than infrastructure-generic, creating tight coupling to business logic
commands/constants.ts mixing configuration with command logic - extract to centralized configuration management
CLI importing agent/lifecycle/loop.ts creates architectural layer violation - implement service interface to decouple CLI from agent internals
cli/errors.ts contains domain-specific error types rather than infrastructure errors - creates tight coupling between presentation and business layers

zones.name: Hench 4
insights: Zone handles security through path/command validation before tool execution
Central dispatch pattern routes all tool calls through single entry point
Good separation between security checks (guard) and tool implementations
Strong cohesion (0.68) with proper separation between security validation and tool dispatch logic
Guard rails provide comprehensive protection against path traversal and command injection
Backward compatibility shim in agent/tools.ts maintains API stability during refactoring
Acts as security gateway for entire tool ecosystem with no bypass mechanisms
Implements defense-in-depth with both path validation and command filtering
Security zone properly gates all tool access without creating backdoors or escape hatches
Zone implements fail-safe security model where denied operations throw exceptions rather than returning error codes, preventing accidental bypass through error handling bugs
Security validation occurs at multiple granularities (command-level, path-level, argument-level) creating defense-in-depth without performance penalty
Guard implementation uses allowlist approach exclusively - no blacklist patterns that could be circumvented
Consider adding structured error types for different validation failures to enable better error handling in consuming zones

zones.name: Hench 5
insights: Zone groups tools with specialized command-line interfaces (git, test runners)
Moderate cohesion (0.5) suggests tools could be more tightly integrated
Each tool handles its own command parsing and execution logic
Moderate cohesion (0.5) and coupling (0.5) indicates loosely related tools that could benefit from stronger unifying patterns
Git tool properly restricts allowed subcommands for security
Test runner provides automatic detection of test frameworks and scoped execution
Operates as leaf nodes in tool architecture with minimal cross-dependencies
Each tool maintains isolated command surface without shared state
Clean consumer relationship with tool-security zone - accepts validation without coupling to implementation
Test runner and git tools share no common interface despite similar command-line execution patterns
Tool implementations lack standardized error handling or result formatting conventions
Each specialized tool implements its own command parsing and execution without shared abstractions, leading to inconsistent interfaces and duplicated validation logic
Tools implement divergent error handling strategies - git tool throws exceptions while test-runner returns result objects with error flags
Command parsing logic duplicated across git.ts and test-runner.ts without shared validation utilities
Each tool maintains its own subprocess management approach creating inconsistent behavior for timeouts and cancellation
Extract common command execution interface to standardize error handling and subprocess management across all specialized tools
Consolidate duplicate argument parsing logic between git.ts and test-runner.ts into shared utility module

zones.name: Rex PRD Management CLI
zones.description: A self-contained CLI tool and MCP server for managing hierarchical product requirements documents, including code analysis, tree operations, validation, persistence, and AI-agent workflow integration.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Contains 71% of project files (36/51) — may be too broad, consider splitting
The dependency graph flows cleanly from schema -> core -> store -> cli with no circular dependencies, indicating strong architectural discipline for a 36-file single-zone codebase.
The analyze module (scanners, propose, reconcile) is a well-isolated subsystem that could be extracted into its own zone if the project grows, since it has minimal coupling to core.
The MCP server in cli/mcp.ts at ~405 lines is the largest single file and serves as a second entry point alongside the CLI dispatcher, making it a natural seam for future zone splitting.

The store layer (file-adapter.ts) imports core/tree.js and core/canonical.js, creating a dependency inversion: store depends on core business logic instead of being a dumb persistence layer. Layering should be schema ← core ← store, but store reaches sideways into core.
PRIORITY_ORDER is duplicated three times (core/canonical.ts, core/next-task.ts, analyze/propose.ts) — a hidden coupling via copy-paste that risks drift if priority tiers change.
CLI commands init.ts and validate.ts bypass the store abstraction by directly using node:fs/promises for config file I/O, undermining the adapter pattern.
The core layer has no barrel export (no src/core/index.ts), so consumers import from individual files (core/tree.js, core/next-task.js), making the public API surface implicit rather than explicit.
REX_DIR constant is duplicated: declared in cli/commands/constants.ts and re-declared in cli/mcp.ts, creating a sync risk for the two entry points.

sortByPriority logic is duplicated between core/canonical.ts:14-27 (sortItems, recursive) and core/next-task.ts:11-18 (sortByPriority, shallow) — two subtly different sort implementations within the same core layer that can diverge.

MCP tool handlers in cli/mcp.ts reimplement the same orchestration logic as CLI commands (e.g., get_next_task duplicates cmdNext, update_task_status duplicates cmdUpdate) rather than sharing a common service layer, creating a parallel maintenance burden across ~400 lines.

The PRDItem interface in schema/v1.ts:21 has an index signature `[key: string]: unknown` alongside explicit fields, which defeats TypeScript's excess-property checking and allows arbitrary data to pass type checks silently — the `.passthrough()` on the Zod schema compounds this by accepting unknown keys at runtime too.

readLog() at store/file-adapter.ts:105 casts each parsed JSON line as LogEntry without validation (`JSON.parse(line) as LogEntry`), while appendLog() validates via Zod before writing — asymmetric serialization boundary means corrupted log files can inject malformed entries into the system.

The analyze --accept flow in cli/commands/analyze.ts:125-169 calls store.addItem() sequentially in a loop (one file write per item) with no batching or transaction, so a failure mid-loop leaves the PRD in a partially-updated state with no rollback.

update.ts:6-12 hardcodes VALID_STATUSES and VALID_PRIORITIES as string Sets instead of deriving them from the Zod enums in schema/validate.ts (ItemStatusSchema, PrioritySchema), creating a second source of truth that will drift if enum values change.
callSourceVisionMCP in recommend.ts:33-78 uses raw child_process.spawn with callback-based stream handling, but never sets a timeout — a hung sourcevision process will block the CLI indefinitely.

The PRDItemSchema in validate.ts:18 is typed as `z.ZodType<Record<string, unknown>>` erasing the inferred Zod type, so validateDocument returns `Record<string, unknown>` forcing every callsite to cast `as PRDDocument` — the Zod schema is well-typed internally but the export discards the type information.

PRIORITY_ORDER is defined three times (canonical.ts:7, next-task.ts:4, propose.ts:25) with two different type signatures — propose.ts uses Record<string, number> (loose) while the other two use Record<Priority, number> (strict), creating a silent type-safety gap
The adapter field in RexConfig is dead configuration — all 8 createStore() call sites hardcode the literal string "file" instead of reading config.adapter, making alternative adapters unreachable

Log event names for the same semantic operation differ between CLI and MCP: update.ts:68 emits "item_updated" while mcp.ts:129 emits "status_changed" for status changes, with no shared event-name constants
init.ts and validate.ts both bypass the store abstraction layer, using raw fs readFile/writeFile directly, making them incompatible with any non-file adapter

zones.name: Analysis Engine
zones.description: Core analysis pipeline including file inventory, import graph extraction, Louvain community detection, route parsing, AI enrichment, and CLI orchestration.
insights: High cohesion (0.94) — files are tightly interconnected
High cohesion (0.94) reflects a well-structured pipeline where analyzers, CLI commands, schema constants, and utilities form a tight internal dependency chain.
zones.ts orchestrates Louvain clustering, AI enrichment, and insight generation—consider splitting algorithmic and AI-enrichment concerns for testability.
The CLI commands/ directory acts as the orchestration layer calling all analyzer phases sequentially, making it the natural entry point for the entire tool.
src/util/ (sort.ts, merge.ts) is exclusively consumed by analyzers—this utility layer has zero viewer or test imports, reinforcing its role as an analyzer-private subsystem.
All analyzers accept typed parameters and return values rather than reading/writing JSON directly; the CLI commands layer handles all file I/O, keeping analyzers pure and composable.

enrich.ts (925 lines) has 7-8 distinct responsibilities: prompt construction, AI process lifecycle, JSON parsing, finding extraction, batch orchestration, meta-evaluation, and result merging — it should be split into at least 4 focused modules.
enrich.ts accepts `inventory` and `imports` parameters that are never read inside the function body — these are dead parameters that mislead callers about the function's data dependencies.
enrich.ts contains dead code at line 815-816: a ternary expression `(condition ? undefined : undefined)` that always evaluates to `undefined` regardless of branch.
next-steps.ts is a new untracked analyzer with zero test coverage — no unit, integration, or e2e tests exist for it anywhere in the test suite.
Rate-limit errors from the AI CLI are detected but trigger immediate retry with no backoff delay, burning through all 3 attempts instantly before giving up.
SV_DIR and TOOL_VERSION are independently defined in 3 files (cli/commands/constants.ts, analyzers/manifest.ts, cli/mcp.ts) — version drift risk if any is updated without the others

detectCirculars is implemented twice with near-identical DFS logic: src/analyzers/imports.ts:232 and src/util/merge.ts:129 — the imports.ts comment references merge.ts but re-implements rather than importing
computeSummary (inventory.ts:641) and computeInventorySummary (merge.ts:36) are functionally identical with different names — classic DRY violation

GENERIC_PREFIXES (inventory.ts:345) and GENERIC_SEGMENTS (zones.ts:29) serve the same purpose but differ: zones includes 'pkg', inventory does not — a file under pkg/ gets inconsistent category vs zone ID

enrich.ts (925 lines) is a god module mixing 7 responsibilities: pass config, prompt building, timeout computation, Claude CLI invocation, JSON parsing, finding extraction, batch orchestration + retry — highest maintenance burden in the codebase
_updatedFindings bypasses the EnrichResult interface via 'as' casts in enrich.ts:703 and zones.ts:496 — type safety escape hatch instead of extending the interface properly
metaEvaluationCount exists in schema/v1.ts:154 but is absent from the Zod validation schema in schema/validate.ts — writing succeeds but strict validation would strip or reject it
commands/analyze.ts (313 lines) imports from 8+ modules and repeats a 4-phase pattern of readFileSync→JSON.parse→analyze→writeFileSync — orchestration is monolithic with high cascade risk
Barrel export src/analyzers/index.ts omits enrich, louvain, context, llms-txt, and next-steps — false expectation of completeness, and no consumer actually imports via the barrel

zones.name: Interactive Viewer
zones.description: Preact single-page application that visualizes analysis results through force-directed graphs, zone maps, route trees, and findings panels, consuming only schema types with zero analyzer dependencies.
insights: High cohesion (0.96) — files are tightly interconnected
Contains 43% of project files (46/107) — may be too broad, consider splitting
Contains 43% of project files (46/106) — may be too broad, consider splitting
Highest cohesion (0.96) and lowest coupling (0.04) confirm the viewer is fully decoupled from the analysis engine, communicating only through serialized JSON conforming to schema/v1.ts.
The component/view separation follows a clean hierarchy: views import components, components are pure props-driven, and loader.ts is the single data ingestion point.
schema-compat.ts provides forward/backward compatibility for data format changes, which is essential since the viewer may load data produced by different analyzer versions.

The viewer imports schema/validate.ts directly (not through the barrel index.ts), creating an implicit dependency on Zod at bundle time—validate.ts and its Zod dependency are compiled into the viewer bundle even though schema/index.ts re-exports the same symbols.

The viewer's separate esbuild entry point (build.js) with Preact JSX transform means it cannot share compiled artifacts with the Node-side CLI/analyzer code—changes to shared schema types require rebuilding both targets.

views/graph.ts (628 lines) contains a 436-line imperative renderGraph function that manually constructs SVG DOM, binds event listeners, and manages mutable closure state — the physics extraction into graph/ was started but rendering was never extracted alongside it.

The LOD rendering logic (circle radius clamping, font-size formula, label visibility) is duplicated verbatim between the tick callback (lines 365-390) and the wheel handler (lines 420-441) in graph.ts.
The `meterClass` function is defined identically in both views/zones.ts and components/detail-panel.ts with the same 0.7/0.4 threshold constants — a simple copy-paste that should be a shared utility.
The `basename` extraction pattern (`.split('/').pop() || x`) appears 11 times across 6 viewer files — a missing utility function.
sidebar.ts inlines two full base64-encoded PNG logos as JavaScript string constants, bloating the component and preventing image optimization or lazy-loading.
View export naming is inconsistent: Overview and Graph lack the 'View' suffix used by all 6 other views (ZonesView, FilesView, ArchitectureView, ProblemsView, SuggestionsView, RoutesView)
ZONE_COLORS[i % ZONE_COLORS.length] modulo-index pattern is repeated 9 times across 5 files — no shared getZoneColor() utility exists

graph.ts at 627 lines is a split-brain architecture: Preact component (lines 38-189) manages state while renderGraph() (lines 192-627) does imperative SVG DOM creation with deeply interleaved closure-scoped mutable state

Record<string, unknown> is used as the detail panel data contract across 8 call sites in 5 files — relies on duck-typing (typeof detail.path === 'string' && !detail.zoneId) instead of a discriminated union
fileToZone map construction is independently implemented in graph.ts, files.ts, and detail-panel.ts — same iteration over zones.zones with slightly different shapes
FlowDiagram data preparation (pairCounts from crossings) is copy-pasted identically between views/zones.ts:59-78 and views/architecture.ts:54-74
.split('/').pop() || path appears 11 times across 7 files as ad-hoc basename extraction — no shared utility

CSS has unused classes: .zone-insights (cards.css:88-113), .component-tree/.component-node (cards.css:258-287), .route-node (routes.css:10-20), .route-layout (routes.css:52-56) — never referenced from TypeScript

zones.name: Test Fixtures
zones.description: Self-contained sample TypeScript project used as input data for integration and end-to-end tests, intentionally isolated with no imports from or exports to production code.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling (0.0) confirm this is a fully self-contained fixture project that exists solely as test input data.
No entry points means no production code imports these files—they are consumed only by test runners scanning the fixtures/ directory path.
The small-ts-project fixture models a realistic micro-application (models, services, utils, config) providing representative input for analyzer pipeline tests.
small-ts-project fixture (7 files) lacks edge cases: no circular dependencies, no binary files, no deeply nested directories, no non-TypeScript sources
remix-app fixture (5 files) only covers file-based routes — no config-based routes, no splat routes, no optional segments, no error boundaries
Fixtures are only used by integration/e2e tests; all unit tests create their own inline temp directories and files rather than referencing the fixture directories

zones.name: Test Suite
zones.description: Unit, integration, and end-to-end tests covering analyzer logic, schema validation, CLI workflows, and the serve command, with imports reaching into both analyzer and viewer zones.
insights: High coupling (0.54) — 4 imports target "analysis-engine"
Low cohesion (0.46) is expected for a test zone—tests naturally fan out across the codebase to import the modules they verify rather than forming tight internal clusters.
High coupling (0.54) correctly reflects that tests are consumers of production code; the 4 imports into analyzers and 1 into viewer match the test coverage distribution.

schema/validate.ts landing in this zone suggests it is primarily exercised by tests rather than production code paths—consider importing it in the CLI validate command to strengthen its production-zone gravity.

validate.ts is now imported in both the CLI validate command and the viewer loader, contradicting its previous test-zone gravity—it belongs logically in the schema layer shared by all three production zones.
The `renderToDiv` helper function is copy-pasted identically across all 5 viewer test files instead of being extracted to a shared test utility module.

Test data factory functions (makeFileEntry, makeInventory, makeEdge, makeImports) exist only in zones.test.ts while integration tests for llms-txt and context manually construct the same data structures verbosely.

The integration tests for llms-txt.ts and context.ts use synthetic inline data objects rather than running the actual pipeline — they are effectively unit tests misplaced in the integration directory.
zones.test.ts (1265 lines) is a mega-test spanning 3 source modules (zones.ts, louvain.ts, enrich.ts) — no dedicated louvain.test.ts or enrich.test.ts exist
components.test.ts (696 lines) tests both components.ts and route-detection.ts — no route-detection.test.ts exists, making route detection tests hard to discover
Core graph algorithms louvainPhase1, mergeBidirectionalCoupling, and capZoneCount have zero direct unit tests — only exercised indirectly through integration-level analyzeZones tests
Pure functions tryParseJSON, buildMetaPrompt, and getPassConfig in enrich.ts are trivially testable but completely untested
validateImports and validateZones are imported in validate.test.ts but have zero test cases — malformed data could silently pass validation

zones.name: Root
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Perfect cohesion indicates clear separation between CLI orchestration, CI pipeline, configuration management, and web server coordination
Zero coupling suggests this zone serves as a pure coordination layer without tight dependencies
Four focused entry points (ci, cli, config, web) provide clean interface boundaries for the monorepo
Excellent architectural design with perfect cohesion and zero coupling
Serves as a clean facade pattern, providing unified entry points while delegating to specialized packages
Implements facade pattern with four distinct entry points (ci, cli, config, web) that coordinate package interactions without creating dependencies
File naming convention breaks at config.js which handles unified configuration rather than orchestration, suggesting potential misclassification
web.js implements daemon process management (PID files, background mode) which is infrastructure concern rather than pure orchestration
config.js implements cross-package configuration management rather than orchestration - consider renaming zone to 'coordination-layer' or moving config.js to separate zone
Hub: packages/hench/src/tools/rex.ts is imported by 3 zones — cross-cutting dependency
Bidirectional coupling: "hench" ↔ "hench-3" (9+21 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface

Bidirectional coupling: "specialized-tools" ↔ "tool-security" (3+2 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface

Bidirectional coupling: "hench-4" ↔ "hench-5" (2+3 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Size imbalance: largest zone has 27 files vs smallest with 4 — uneven decomposition
The hench package demonstrates excellent modular design with five distinct zones handling core logic, completion, CLI, and specialized concerns
Strong cross-zone communication patterns (hench-3 ↔ hench with 30 total imports) indicate proper layering between CLI and core functionality
Zero coupling in root orchestration and claude-client zones shows excellent architectural boundaries
Strong security-first architecture with guardrails protecting all tool execution paths
Clear layering from security validation → dispatch → specialized implementation
Tool system exhibits good extensibility with consistent patterns for adding new capabilities
Exceptionally well-architected codebase with strong zone cohesion across all analyzed components
Clean separation of concerns between orchestration, client libraries, and agent subsystems
High interconnectivity between hench zones (21 imports hench-3→hench, 9 reverse) suggests tight coupling in agent subsystem
Tool dispatch architecture properly isolates security concerns from implementation details
Bidirectional imports between zones may indicate circular dependencies that could complicate testing and maintenance
Layered architecture emerges with orchestration → CLI → agent core → specialized tools flow
Multiple adapter patterns (Claude providers, tool dispatch) provide clean abstraction boundaries
Command pattern appears consistently across CLI infrastructure and task completion zones
Tool architecture follows secure-by-design layered pattern: security validation → dispatch → specialized implementation
Cross-zone tool communication flows unidirectionally through central dispatch without backflow
Security zone acts as architectural chokepoint ensuring no tool execution bypasses validation
Clear layered architecture with orchestration layer at top, CLI infrastructure in middle, and agent core at bottom provides good separation of concerns
Hub-and-spoke pattern centered on packages/hench/src/tools/rex.ts creates single point of dependency across multiple zones
Missing interface abstraction between agent-core and cli-infrastructure zones leads to tight coupling through direct imports
Tool ecosystem demonstrates proper layered security architecture with no privilege escalation paths
Clear separation between security concerns (tool-security) and functionality (specialized-tools) with well-defined interfaces
Agent-core and cli-infrastructure zones exhibit architectural layer violation where presentation layer (CLI) directly depends on business logic internals
Missing service layer between CLI commands and agent lifecycle creates maintenance burden and testing complexity
Token management concerns scattered across multiple zones indicate need for centralized token service abstraction
Tool architecture exhibits asymmetric security model where specialized-tools zone trusts tool-security validation completely without implementing any internal safeguards
Cross-zone tool communication creates implicit dependency chain where changes in tool-security validation rules could break specialized-tools implementations without compile-time detection
The hub pattern around packages/hench/src/tools/rex.ts suggests potential violation of single responsibility principle as it serves as both domain logic and architectural coordination point
CLI commands bypass service layer to directly manipulate agent lifecycle internals, violating clean architecture principles
Token management logic distributed across agent-core lifecycle files and task-completion validation without centralized abstraction
Tool ecosystem lacks standardized interface contract between security validation and tool implementation layers, creating fragile coupling through implicit assumptions about validation behavior

Multiple bidirectional coupling patterns between hench zones (hench↔hench-3, hench-3↔hench-4, hench-4↔hench-5) create circular dependency chains that complicate module loading and increase risk of deadlocks
Zone naming conventions inconsistent - orchestration-layer contains non-orchestration files while agent-core mixes multiple architectural concerns
Resource management (token budgets, usage tracking) scattered across agent-core and task-completion zones without centralized service abstraction
Validation responsibilities fragmented across multiple zones (task-completion, cli-infrastructure) creating unclear ownership boundaries
Testing conventions vary significantly across zones with no apparent standardization strategy
Tool ecosystem exhibits split-brain architecture where tool-security zone enforces strict validation while specialized-tools zone implements ad-hoc validation creating redundant security layers
Cross-zone tool invocation path creates mandatory serialization through dispatch layer even for tools that could operate independently, introducing unnecessary performance overhead
Security model assumes all specialized tools are equally privileged despite git operations being inherently more dangerous than test execution
Tool architecture lacks circuit breaker pattern - failed security validations in one tool don't prevent continued execution of other tools in same session
Establish consistent zone naming convention - current zones mix functional (task-completion) and architectural (cli-infrastructure) naming styles
Create centralized resource-management zone to consolidate token budget, usage tracking, and other resource concerns scattered across agent-core and task-completion
Standardize validation architecture - currently split across task-completion/validation and cli-infrastructure creates unclear responsibility ownership
Implement privilege differentiation in tool-security zone to apply stricter validation for high-risk tools like git operations while allowing streamlined validation for low-risk tools
Add tool execution session management to track validation failures and implement progressive restrictions for tools that repeatedly fail security checks
Consider bypassing dispatch layer for tools that require no external resources (like pure computation tools) to reduce serialization overhead
Circular dependency chains form a systemic architectural anti-pattern throughout the hench subsystem, requiring comprehensive refactoring to establish proper layered architecture
Resource management concerns (tokens, validation, lifecycle) are distributed across 4+ zones without centralized coordination, creating maintenance complexity
The codebase exhibits a dual personality: excellent separation of concerns at the package level, but problematic tight coupling within the hench agent subsystem
Agent subsystem violates dependency inversion principle - high-level modules (cli-infrastructure, orchestration-layer) depend directly on low-level modules (agent-core) instead of abstractions
Hub-spoke topology around rex.ts combined with bidirectional hench zone coupling creates dependency graph cycles that will cause module loading failures in strict dependency injection systems
Implement strict layering: orchestration-layer → service-interfaces → agent-core, with cli-infrastructure consuming only service-interfaces to eliminate direct coupling
Consolidate resource management into dedicated resource-management zone containing token budgets (from agent-core), usage tracking (from task-completion), and validation state (from tool-security)
Dependency inversion violation is systemic - multiple findings (15, 25, 28) confirm CLI bypasses abstractions to directly manipulate agent internals
Circular dependency crisis - bidirectional coupling patterns (0, 1, 2, 16) combined with hub topology (3, 13) create module loading deadlock risk
Resource management fragmentation - token budgets, usage tracking, and validation state scattered across 4 zones without coordination
Critical architectural debt: circular dependency chains (hench zones) + hub topology (rex.ts) + dependency inversion violations (CLI→agent-core) form triple threat to module loading stability
Immediate action required: Create service-interfaces zone containing AgentService, ResourceManager, and ToolRegistry abstractions to break circular dependencies before they cause runtime failures
Zone naming inconsistency reveals architectural confusion - mixing domain concepts (task-completion) with technical layers (cli-infrastructure) indicates unclear separation of concerns
